questions.question: State and prove the necessary and sufficient conditions for a connected graph to have an Eulerian circuit.
keyPoints: Every vertex must have even degree
Use handshaking lemma: sum of degrees = 2E
If Eulerian circuit exists, each time we enter/leave a vertex contributes 2 to degree
Conversely, if all vertices have even degree, construct circuit using Hierholzer's algorithm
Connected graph is required for traversal

questions.question: Explain the difference between Eulerian and Hamiltonian paths with examples.
keyPoints: Eulerian path visits every edge exactly once; Hamiltonian path visits every vertex exactly once
Triangle graph: Eulerian (all edges) but not Hamiltonian cycle as it repeats vertices
Complete graph K4: Has both Eulerian circuit and Hamiltonian cycle
Path graph P3: Has Eulerian path but no Hamiltonian cycle
These are fundamentally different problems with different conditions

questions.question: State Dirac's Theorem and give an example to verify it.
keyPoints: Dirac's Theorem: If minimum degree ≥ n/2 for n ≥ 3, graph is Hamiltonian
K4: minimum degree = 3, n/2 = 2, so condition satisfied; K4 is Hamiltonian
Counterpoint: Ore's Theorem is a generalization
Theorem provides sufficient but not necessary condition
Minimum degree 2 is necessary but not sufficient

questions.question: Apply Fleury's Algorithm to find an Eulerian path in the graph: vertices A,B,C,D with edges AB, BC, CD, DA, AC.
keyPoints: Degrees: A=3, B=2, C=3, D=2 - has exactly two odd vertices (A,C)
Start from vertex with odd degree: A or C
From A: choose edge not a bridge initially - avoid AC initially as it's a bridge
One valid Eulerian path: A→B→C→D→A→C
Verify all 5 edges visited exactly once
Path starts at A (odd degree), ends at C (odd degree)

questions.question: Define semi-Eulerian and semi-Hamiltonian graphs with one example each.
keyPoints: Semi-Eulerian: Has Eulerian path but not circuit - exactly two vertices of odd degree
Example: Path graph Pn has Eulerian path but not circuit
Semi-Hamiltonian: Has Hamiltonian path but not necessarily cycle
Example: Path graph Pn has Hamiltonian path covering all vertices
Note: Every Hamiltonian cycle gives a Hamiltonian path, but not vice versa

questions.question: Explain the relationship between the Knight's Tour problem and Hamiltonian paths.
keyPoints: Knight's Tour: Find a path that visits every square of chessboard exactly once
Represents a Hamiltonian path problem on the knight's move graph
64 vertices (squares), edges represent valid knight moves
Closed Knight's Tour: Hamiltonian cycle; Open Knight's Tour: Hamiltonian path
This is NP-complete, like general Hamiltonian path problem

questions.question: For the graph given below, determine whether Eulerian path/circuit exists. If yes, find one using Fleury's Algorithm: Vertices: {A,B,C,D,E}, Edges: {AB, BC, CD, DE, EA, AC, AD}.
keyPoints: Calculate degrees: A=3, B=2, C=2, D=2, E=3
Two vertices have odd degree (A and E) - semi-Eulerian
Eulerian path exists but not circuit
Start from odd degree vertex (A or E)
Apply Fleury's Algorithm: avoid bridges initially
One valid path: A→B→C→D→E→A→D→E or A→E→D→C→B→A→D→E
Verify all 7 edges visited exactly once

questions.question: Prove that if a connected graph has exactly two vertices of odd degree, then it has an Eulerian path (but not circuit).
keyPoints: Let u and v be the two odd-degree vertices
Add edge uv to make all degrees even
New graph has Eulerian circuit by previous theorem
Remove edge uv from the circuit
Now we have a trail from u to v covering all edges
This is an Eulerian path from u to v
Since u and v are distinct, cannot be a circuit
Path uses every edge exactly once by construction

questions.question: Compare Eulerian and Hamiltonian paths. Also, discuss why finding Hamiltonian paths is computationally harder than finding Eulerian paths.
keyPoints: Eulerian: visits every edge once; Hamiltonian: visits every vertex once
Eulerian conditions are simple (all even or two odd degrees) - O(V+E) algorithm exists
Hamiltonian problem is NP-complete - no polynomial algorithm known
Eulerian can be solved in linear time; Hamiltonian requires exponential time in worst case
Dirac's and Ore's theorems give sufficient conditions but not necessary
Practical applications: TSP, routing, scheduling all use Hamiltonian paths
No simple characterization exists for Hamiltonian graphs like Eulerian graphs

questions.question: Design an algorithm using backtracking to find a Hamiltonian path in a graph. Analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Start from arbitrary vertex, mark it as visited
At each step, try all unvisited neighbors recursively
If all vertices visited, success; backtrack if dead end
Time complexity: O(n!) in worst case (try all permutations)
Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack and visited array
Optimization: Pruning using degree checks, connectivity tests
Comparison: Hierholzer's algorithm for Eulerian is O(V+E) - much faster
